message.content:
I'm designing a hybrid CPU simulator that combines QEMU (fast functional emulation) with Verilator (cycle-accurate RTL simulation) for RISC-V. The goal: run uninteresting code regions (boot, libc init, OS setup) in QEMU at full speed, then hand off to Verilator for "interesting" code regions (kernels under study) where cycle-accurate pipeline behavior matters. Then optionally hand back to QEMU.

The CORE technical challenge is bidirectional CPU state exchange:
- Architectural registers (32 GPR + FP regs + CSRs) - this is straightforward
- Memory state - the hard part: how do we share/sync memory between QEMU's softmmu/TLB and Verilator's RTL memory model?
- Privilege state, MMU/TLB, interrupt pending bits, etc.

Research existing prior art. Search for and summarize:

1. **QEMU's "TCG plugin" interface** and `qemu-plugin.h` - can plugins read/write guest registers and memory? Can they pause execution at a PC?

2. **QEMU's gdbstub** - this is a well-known way to read/write all CPU state via a remote protocol. Can it be driven programmatically (not just gdb)? What state does it expose for RISC-V?

3. **QEMU's `-icount` mode and replay** - relevant?

4. **Real systems that do hybrid sim**: gem5's "fast forward" with KVM/atomic CPU then switch to detailed (O3/Minor) CPU - how do they handle the switch? gem5's checkpoint/restore. Spike + Verilator co-sim (like dromajo, "tandem verification"). Chipyard FireSim. Any RISC-V specific hybrid simulators?

5. **Memory consistency**: When QEMU runs and modifies RAM, then Verilator takes over, how do you transfer the dirty RAM contents? Options: full memcpy of guest RAM (4GB?!), shared mmap, dirty-page tracking, COW. What does QEMU expose?

6. **PC trigger mechanism**: How would QEMU stop at a designated PC ("magic instruction" / breakpoint / hcall) to trigger handoff? Is there a `csrw` or `ecall` convention used in industry for this?

7. **Andes RISC-V QEMU fork**: User mentioned a tarball "andescycle-dev-v0.16-17-g03dfe23.tar.gz" in /local/nick/vsim - could this be Andes' own QEMU? If you can determine from web search what andescycle is, report it.

Return a structured summary (under 700 words) covering: (a) which existing mechanisms are most promising, (b) concrete APIs/headers to use, (c) the recommended architecture (which side is "master", how state moves), (d) failure modes to watch for. Cite QEMU source files / docs by path where possible.

content.text: I'll research this systematically. Let me start by examining what's locally available and then search the web for prior art.

Web search results for query: "QEMU TCG plugin qemu-plugin.h read guest registers memory API capabilities"

Links: [{"title":"QEMU TCG Plugins — QEMU documentation","url":"https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/devel/tcg-plugins.html"},{"title":"QEMU TCG Plugins — QEMU 10.0.94 documentation","url":"https://qemu-stsquad.readthedocs.io/en/latest/devel/tcg-plugins.html"},{"title":"Qemu TCG Plugins - how to access guest registers","url":"https://lists.nongnu.org/archive/html/qemu-devel/2020-03/msg08132.html"},{"title":"[PATCH v2] TCG plugin API extension to read guest memory content by an address","url":"https://www.mail-archive.com/qemu-devel@nongnu.org/msg941762.html"},{"title":"QEMU TCG Plugins — QEMU 8.1.5 documentation","url":"https://qemu.readthedocs.io/en/v8.1.5/devel/tcg-plugins.html"},{"title":"Re: Qemu TCG Plugins - how to access guest registers?","url":"https://lists.nongnu.org/archive/html/qemu-discuss/2020-03/msg00038.html"},{"title":"QEMU TCG Plugins — QEMU documentation","url":"https://virtio-fs.gitlab.io/qemu/devel/tcg-plugins.html"},{"title":"Re: Qemu TCG Plugins - how to access guest registers?","url":"https://www.mail-archive.com/qemu-devel@nongnu.org/msg693089.html"},{"title":"Add api to read CPU registers in TCG plugins | Patchew","url":"https://patchew.org/QEMU/20230803112551.14803-1-m.tyutin@yadro.com/"},{"title":"Allow TCG plugins to read memory (#1719) · Issues · QEMU / QEMU · GitLab","url":"https://gitlab.com/qemu-project/qemu/-/issues/1719"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about QEMU TCG plugin capabilities for reading guest registers and memory through the qemu-plugin.h API:

## Memory Access Capabilities

The `qemu_plugin_read_memory_vaddr()` function reads len bytes of data starting at an address and stores it into a buffer. If the buffer is not large enough to hold len bytes, it will be expanded to the necessary size, reallocating if necessary, and len must be greater than 0. However, this function does not ensure writes are flushed prior to reading, so callers should take care when calling this function in plugin callbacks to avoid attempting to read data which may not yet be written and should use the memory callback API instead.

## Guest Register Access

Interestingly, direct guest register access was initially limited in the TCG plugin API. Register access is a tricky problem because of the fact that QEMU supports so many guest architectures, and the developers weren't keen on implementing a functional but ugly API that hard-coded values like gdb register ids.

However, more recent developments added register access capabilities. A newer API includes functions like `qemu_plugin_reg_ptr()` and `qemu_plugin_reg_size()` to query register data pointers and sizes in a register context, and `qemu_plugin_regs_load()` to load data from all registers and store them in context.

## Memory Callback Registration

The `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_mem_cb()` function registers a full callback for every memory access generated by an instruction.

Links: [{"title":"gem5: X86 Full-System Tutorial","url":"https://www.gem5.org/documentation/gem5-stdlib/x86-full-system-tutorial"},{"title":"gem5: Out of order CPU model","url":"https://www.gem5.org/documentation/general_docs/cpu_models/O3CPU"},{"title":"gem5: Checkpoints","url":"https://www.gem5.org/documentation/general_docs/checkpoints/"},{"title":"M5Ops · gem5 · Discussion #1214","url":"https://github.com/orgs/gem5/discussions/1214"},{"title":"gem5: X86 Linux Boot Status on gem5-19","url":"https://www.gem5.org/project/2020/03/09/boot-tests.html"},{"title":"gem5: Full System AMD GPU model","url":"https://www.gem5.org/documentation/general_docs/gpu_models/gpufs"},{"title":"gem5: More complex configuration script","url":"https://courses.grainger.illinois.edu/cs433/gem5/part1/cache_config/"},{"title":"parti-gem5: gem5’s Timing Mode Parallelised","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2308.09445v2"},{"title":"1 Dist-gem5 Architecture","url":"http://publish.illinois.edu/icsl-pdgem5/files/2015/12/dist-gem5-arch-v4.pdf"},{"title":"Gem5 - Lele's Memo","url":"https://cnlelema.github.io/memo/en/arch/basics/gem5/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about gem5's fast forward, KVM, atomic switch, detailed O3 CPU, checkpoint, and memory capabilities:

## KVM Fast-Forward and Core Switching

gem5's ability allows booting of the operating system in KVM fast-forward mode and switching to a detailed CPU model to run the benchmark. gem5's SimpleSwitchableProcessor can be used for simulations in which a user wants to switch out one type of core for another during a simulation.

## CPU Models

The O3CPU is an out of order CPU model loosely based on the Alpha 21264. kvmCPU is a CPU that does not do any timing simulation, but rather uses actual hardware to run the simulated code and is mainly used for fast forwarding. AtomicSimpleCPU also does not do any timing simulation and uses atomic memory accesses, mostly used for fast forwarding and cache warming.

## Checkpointing

By default, gem5 assumes that the checkpoint is to be restored using Atomic CPUs, but this may not work if the checkpoint was recorded using Timing/Detailed/Inorder CPU, and one can mention the option --restore-with-cpu <CPU Type> on the command line.

## KVM to O3 CPU Switching Workflow

One solution is to boot with KVM, switch to atomic CPU (using memory mapped calls), run till the beginning of ROI, and then switch the CPU to O3 (using magic instructions).

Web search results for query: "QEMU gdbstub programmatic interface RISC-V CSR register access remote protocol"

Links: [{"title":"RISC-V Bytes: Cross-Platform Debugging with QEMU and GDB · Daniel Mangum","url":"https://danielmangum.com/posts/risc-v-bytes-qemu-gdb/"},{"title":"GDB usage — QEMU documentation","url":"https://qemu-project.gitlab.io/qemu/system/gdb.html"},{"title":"[Qemu-devel] [PATCH v8 27/35] RISC-V: Implement modular CSR helper inter","url":"https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/qemu-devel/2018-04/msg04522.html"},{"title":"[v1] target/riscv: Various fixes to gdbstub and CSR access | Patchew","url":"https://patchew.org/QEMU/20230213180215.1524938-1-bmeng@tinylab.org/"},{"title":"Read/write of CSR registers via GDB doesn't work · Issue #156 · riscvarchive/riscv-qemu","url":"https://github.com/riscvarchive/riscv-qemu/issues/156"},{"title":"Debug RISC-V assembly with QEMU and GDB","url":"https://issues.genenetwork.org/topics/riscv/debug-riscv-assembly-with-qemu-and-gdb"},{"title":"Hello, RISC-V and QEMU","url":"https://mth.st/blog/riscv-qemu/"},{"title":"GitHub - bluespec/RISCV_gdbstub: A gdbstub for connecting GDB to a RISC-V Debug Module · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/bluespec/RISCV_gdbstub"},{"title":"qemu/target/riscv/csr.c at master · qemu/qemu","url":"https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/master/target/riscv/csr.c"},{"title":"GDB usage — QEMU 7.2.19 documentation","url":"https://qemu.readthedocs.io/en/v7.2.19/system/gdb.html"}]

I've found relevant search results about QEMU's gdbstub support for RISC-V CSR register access. Here's what the search revealed:

## QEMU GDBStub Support for RISC-V

QEMU supports working with gdb via gdb's remote-connection facility (the "gdbstub"), which allows you to debug guest code in the same way that you might with a low-level debug facility like JTAG on real hardware. You can stop and start the virtual machine, examine state like registers and memory, and set breakpoints and watchpoints.

## RISC-V CSR Register Access

The previous CSR code used csr_read_helper and csr_write_helper functions, but this interface prevented atomic read/modify/write CSR operations and was not safe to be called by target/riscv/gdbstub.c as privilege checks or missing CSRs may call longjmp to generate exceptions.

QEMU moved CSR access into modular read/write functions using a function pointer table for dispatch, and added a read/modify/write interface to support atomic CSR operations and a non-trapping interface to allow exception-free access to CSRs by the debugger.

## Remote Protocol

QEMU supports the gdbstub remote connection protocol, and you can start a gdb server in user-mode QEMU by passing -g <port>. The implementation handles CSR register access through the gdbstub interface, though earlier versions had challenges with register count and XML descriptions for CSR registers.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Andes, AndeSim, and RISC-V cycle-accurate simulators:

## Andes Technology Solutions

Andes Technology provides the AndeSight IDE with simulator options including AndeSim (near-cycle accuracy) and QEMU as part of their software development tools for RISC-V. With AndeSysC™ near-cycle accurate models, designers can perform early profiling during the rapid prototyping phase and obtain high-level performance estimations for the final hardware implementation.

## QEMU and Cycle-Accurate Simulation

The search results reveal that while QEMU is a popular option, it has some limitations for cycle-accurate simulation: QEMU does not simulate the precise timing of CPU cycles. However, researchers have developed cycle-accurate frameworks based on QEMU: QEMU-CAS is the first work that introduces cycle-accurate performance modeling on top of a functional full-system platform for RISC-V ISA.

## Other RISC-V Cycle-Accurate Simulators

The search results also mention several alternatives: MARSS-RISCV consists of detailed cycle-level models of a generic RISC-V in-order and out-of-order processor with a branch prediction unit, TLBs, cache-hierarchy, and comes integrated with DRAMSim3 and Ramulator, which are cycle-accurate DRAM simulators.

The search results didn't contain specific detailed documentation about "andescycle" specifically, but they show that Andes Technology offers near-cycle accurate simulation tools for RISC-V development.

I've completed the web search for your query. Here are the key findings:

## Overview

Dromajo is a RISC-V RV64GC emulator designed for RTL co-simulation. Esperanto is releasing Dromajo to help the RISC-V community.

## Capabilities and Purpose

Dromajo enables executing application (such as benchmarks running on Linux) under fast software simulation, generating checkpoints after a given number of cycles, and resuming such checkpoints for HW/SW co-simulation. It responds to external stimuli like interrupts and debug requests and integrates seamlessly into existing testbench infrastructures.

## Integration with BOOM and Verification

When integrating Dromajo into BOOM infrastructure, the DPI can be called by implementing simple monitor logic in the Reorder Buffer module. Dromajo has been successfully used with the BOOM processor for verification, and Dromajo by itself found a total of nine bugs, with the enhancement of Dromajo with the Logic Fuzzer increasing the exposed bug count to thirteen.

## Co-Simulation with Verilator

Verilator, an open-source tool, converts Verilog designs into cycle-accurate behavioral models in C++, which speeds up simulation and integrates with reference models also written in C++.

The search results show Dromajo is a key tool in the RISC-V verification ecosystem for tandem verification of cores like BOOM through hardware-software co-simulation.

Links: [{"title":"Execution Record/Replay — QEMU documentation","url":"https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/devel/replay.html"},{"title":"Record/replay — QEMU documentation","url":"https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/system/replay.html"},{"title":"TCG Instruction Counting — QEMU documentation","url":"https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/devel/tcg-icount.html"},{"title":"Record/replay — QEMU 8.1.5 documentation","url":"https://qemu.readthedocs.io/en/v8.1.5/system/replay.html"},{"title":"qemu/docs/replay.txt at v2.12.0 · qemu/qemu","url":"https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/v2.12.0/docs/replay.txt"},{"title":"Record/replay — QEMU Debian 1:10.0.6+ds-0+deb13u2 documentation","url":"https://pegaso.changeip.org/DOCS/qemu-system-x86/system/replay.html"},{"title":"Features/record-replay - QEMU","url":"https://wiki.qemu.org/index.php/Features/record-replay"},{"title":"Execution Record/Replay — QEMU 9.2.0 documentation","url":"https://qemu.readthedocs.io/en/v9.2.0/devel/replay.html"},{"title":"TCG Instruction Counting — QEMU 8.2.50 documentation","url":"https://qemu-stsquad.readthedocs.io/en/docs-next/devel/tcg-icount.html"},{"title":"qemu/docs/replay.txt at v5.2.0 · qemu/qemu","url":"https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/v5.2.0/docs/replay.txt"}]

I found comprehensive information about QEMU's icount mode and replay features. Here are the key results:

## QEMU icount Mode and Deterministic Replay

**icount Mode Basics:**
TCG has long supported a feature known as icount which allows for instruction counting during execution. icount was designed to allow deterministic execution in absence of external inputs of the virtual machine. In icount mode virtual clock value is calculated using executed instructions counter. That is why it is completely deterministic and does not have to be recorded.

**Deterministic Execution:**
icount was designed to allow deterministic execution in absence of external inputs of the virtual machine. Record/replay feature is enabled through -icount command-line option, making possible deterministic execution of the machine, interacting with user or network.

**Recording and Replaying:**
The number of instructions elapsed from the last event is written to the log while recording the execution. In replay mode we can predict when to inject that event using the instruction counter. The following non-deterministic data from peripheral devices is saved into the log: mouse and keyboard input, network packets, audio controller input, serial port input, and hardware clocks. Inputs from simulated hardware, memory of VM, software interrupts, and execution of instructions are not saved into the log, because they are deterministic and can be replayed by simulating the behavior of virtual machine starting from initial state.

**Use Cases:**
Record/replay functions are used for the reverse execution and deterministic replay of qemu execution. Determinitsic replay is used to record volatile system execution once and replay it for multiple times for the sake of analysis, debugging, logging, etc.

The search results don't contain specific information about "hybrid simulator handoff" in relation to QEMU's record/replay system.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the relationship between FireSim, Chipyard, QEMU, bootrom, Spike, and RTL:

## FireSim and Chipyard Integration

FireSim has been integrated into Chipyard, which is a framework for hardware designing.

## Workload Simulation Flow

Once you define a workload, the marshal command will produce a corresponding boot-binary and rootfs for each job in the workload. This binary and rootfs can then be launched on qemu or spike (for functional simulation), or installed to a platform for running on real RTL (currently only FireSim is automated).

## Boot Process and Bootrom

The BootROM contains both the first instructions to run when the SoC is powered on as well as the Device Tree Binary (dtb) which details the components of the system. The default bootloader simply loops on a wait-for-interrupt (WFI) instruction as the RISC-V frontend-server (FESVR) loads the actual program. FESVR is a program that runs on the host CPU and can read/write arbitrary parts of the target system memory using the Tethered Serial Interface (TSI). FESVR uses TSI to load a baremetal executable or second-stage bootloader into the SoC memory.

## Debugging Flow with Simulators

The software has already been verified on a functional simulator, such as Spike or QEMU. This indicates a typical workflow where functional simulators (Spike/QEMU) are used before moving to RTL simulation.

The search results indicate a typical development flow: develop/test software on functional simulators (Spike/QEMU), manage workloads with FireMarshal, handle the bootrom configuration, and then move to RTL-level simulation/prototyping with FireSim on FPGAs.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about QEMU memory backend file shared mmap guest RAM dirty page tracking and migration:

## Memory Backend and mmap

Memory inside a "memory-backend" is actually mmapped by RAMBlock through qemu_ram_alloc(), and each RAMBlock has a pointer to the mmap memory and also a ram_addr_t offset. RAM memory regions can be initialized with a mmap-ed backend.

## Shared Memory in Migration

QEMU supports x-ignore-shared VM migration, whereby guest RAM is not migrated using the QEMU migration stream with dirty tracking, but using shared memory / files. It is recommended that guest RAM be backed with some type of shared memory, such as memory-backend-file,share=on, and that the x-ignore-shared capability be set, which combination allows memory to be saved in place.

## Dirty Page Tracking

The live migration feature relies on tracking dirty memory pages so they can be resent if they change during live migration, and there are dirty memory bitmaps for each user in ram_list because dirty memory tracking can be enabled or disabled independently. RAM migration operates in iterative rounds, tracking which pages have been modified (dirtied) by the guest and transmitting them to the destination, with support for multiple optimization strategies including compression, zero page detection, parallel transmission channels (multifd), and postcopy mode.

## Mapped-RAM Migration

For an optimized approach, the mapped-ram feature ensures that RAM pages are mapped directly to offsets in the resulting migration file, enabling multifd threads to write exclusively to those offsets even if the guest is constantly dirtying pages during live migration, and the resulting file will have a bounded size since pages dirtied multiple times will always go to a fixed location.

content.content:
# QEMU Plugin APIs for Register, Memory, and Execution Control

Based on the documentation provided, here are the relevant APIs:

## Register Access (qemu-plugin.h)

**Reading registers:**
```c
bool qemu_plugin_read_register(struct qemu_plugin_register *handle, 
                               GByteArray *buf)
```

**Writing registers:**
```c
bool qemu_plugin_write_register(struct qemu_plugin_register *handle, 
                                GByteArray *buf)
```

## Guest Memory Access (qemu-plugin.h)

**Virtual address reads:**
```c
bool qemu_plugin_read_memory_vaddr(uint64_t addr, GByteArray *data, 
                                   size_t len)
```

**Virtual address writes:**
```c
bool qemu_plugin_write_memory_vaddr(uint64_t addr, GByteArray *data)
```

**Physical address reads (softmmu only):**
```c
enum qemu_plugin_hwaddr_operation_result 
qemu_plugin_read_memory_hwaddr(uint64_t addr, GByteArray *data, size_t len)
```

**Physical address writes (softmmu only):**
```c
enum qemu_plugin_hwaddr_operation_result 
qemu_plugin_write_memory_hwaddr(uint64_t addr, GByteArray *data)
```

## PC-Specific Callbacks

The documentation describes "instruction instrumentation" via `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()`, but no dedicated "install at specific PC" mechanism is documented.

## Execution Control (qemu-plugin.h)

**Redirect execution to a new address:**
```c
void qemu_plugin_set_pc(uint64_t vaddr)
```

Note: This function "does not return" and resumes execution at the specified address.

**No dedicated pause/stop API is documented** in the provided content.

# Dromajo State Handoff to RTL Simulation

Based on the GitHub repository content provided, I cannot find specific details about how Dromajo hands off state to Verilator or other RTL simulators, what checkpoint contents include, memory sharing mechanisms, or DPI functions used.

The documentation mentions that Dromajo is "a RISC-V RV64GC emulator designed for RTL co-simulation" and that it can "generat[e] checkpoints after a given number of cycles, and resum[e] such checkpoints for HW/SW co-simulation." However, the technical implementation details you're asking about—state transfer protocols, checkpoint structure, memory sharing methods, and specific DPI interfaces—are not covered in this repository README or visible file structure.

To find these implementation details, you would need to:
- Review the actual source code in the `src/` directory
- Check the `dromajo_cosim.h` header file mentioned
- Consult detailed documentation in the `doc/` folder
- Examine pull requests and issues that discuss integration specifics

content.content:
# Dromajo CoSim API Extraction

## Function Signatures

**1. Initialization**
```c
dromajo_cosim_state_t *dromajo_cosim_init(int argc, char *argv[]);
```
Creates and initializes the RISC-V ISA golden model state; returns NULL on failure.

**2. Cleanup**
```c
void dromajo_cosim_fini(dromajo_cosim_state_t *state);
```
Destroys state and releases all allocated resources.

**3. Instruction Execution**
```c
int dromajo_cosim_step(dromajo_cosim_state_t *state, int hartid, uint64_t dut_pc, 
                       uint32_t dut_insn, uint64_t dut_wdata, uint64_t mstatus, 
                       bool check);
```
Executes one instruction; returns zero for match/continue, non-zero for termination with exit code in upper bits.

**4. Exception/Interrupt Injection**
```c
void dromajo_cosim_raise_trap(dromajo_cosim_state_t *state, int hartid, 
                              int64_t cause);
```
DUT signals trap occurrence; MSB distinguishes asynchronous interrupts from synchronous exceptions.

**5. Memory Override**
```c
int dromajo_cosim_override_mem(dromajo_cosim_state_t *state, int hartid, 
                               uint64_t dut_paddr, uint64_t dut_val, int size_log2);
```
Allows external devices to write memory directly to Dromajo's model.

**6. Logging Configuration**
```c
void dromajo_install_new_loggers(dromajo_cosim_state_t *state, 
                                 dromajo_logging_func_t *debug_log,
                                 dromajo_logging_func_t *error_log);
```
Configures custom logging/error handlers.

## Handshake Protocol

The host provides: program counter, instruction bits, write-back data, and mstatus. Dromajo overrides mismatches (I/O loads, interrupts, cycle/time/instret CSRs) with expected values, enabling "the model will override with the expected values" for incompatible scenarios.

# Andes QEMU Fork Analysis

Based on the provided GitHub page content, I cannot determine whether this Andes fork includes cycle-accurate features, "andescycle," or hybrid simulation capabilities.

The page displays standard QEMU repository information with the branch `ast-v5_4_1-release` and 354 tags, but the README content shown is the **official QEMU README** with no Andes-specific modifications or feature descriptions.

The header notes: "Official QEMU mirror. Please see http://wiki.qemu.org/Contribute/SubmitAPatch for how to submit changes to QEMU."

**What's missing from this view:**
- No Andes-specific documentation explaining their customizations
- No detailed commit history or branch descriptions visible
- No specialized feature documentation

To understand Andes' modifications, you would need to:
1. Review their actual branch documentation or wiki pages
2. Examine commit messages in the `ast-v5_4_1-release` branch
3. Check for Andes-specific configuration files or documentation directories

The visible content suggests this is primarily a **forked mirror** rather than a heavily documented custom implementation page.

I found relevant documentation about QEMU plugins. Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding your query:

## `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb`

The function `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb` is available with the signature: `void qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb(struct qemu_plugin_insn *insn, qemu_plugin_vcpu_udata_cb_t cb, enum qemu_plugin_cb_flags flags, void *userdata)`.

## Conditional Execution with Breakpoint-Like Functionality

For setting up callbacks tied to specific program counter (PC) values, QEMU provides a related function: `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cond_cb`. The callback function is called when an instruction executes if a condition evaluates to true. If the condition is `QEMU_PLUGIN_COND_ALWAYS`, this function is equivalent to `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb`.

## Note on Breakpoints

The search results also indicate that debug exceptions triggered by architecture-dependent events like breakpoints require particular attention from QEMU to be forwarded to other subsystems such as a GDB server stub. However, the plugin API doesn't directly expose a "stop execution on breakpoint at specific PC" callback. Instead, you would use the conditional execution callback variant to achieve similar functionality by registering a callback that only executes when a specific PC condition is met.

I've performed a web search for your query about QEMU TCG plugin pause execution, stop vcpu_init_cb, interrupt callback, and hand off. Here are the relevant results:

## Key Findings:

**vCPU Initialization and Callbacks:**
The `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_init_cb` function registers a callback that is called every time a vCPU is initialized. There is also a `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_resume_cb` function where the callback is called every time a vCPU resumes execution.

**Interrupt and Execution Control:**
The search results show that asynchronous interrupts will not change control flow mid-block in the TCG plugin system. However, exiting from a translation block will cause the cpu_exec_interrupt() callback to be re-evaluated before executing additional instructions, and it is mandatory to exit this way after any CPU state changes that may unmask interrupts.

**Plugin Lifecycle:**
Plugin uninstallation is asynchronous, meaning callbacks may still occur after the uninstall operation is requested, and the plugin isn't completely uninstalled until the safe work has executed while all vCPUs are quiescent.

The search results provide technical documentation on QEMU's TCG plugin system, though they don't specifically address pausing execution or handing off between callbacks in the exact manner your query suggests.

But the user asked for research findings, not a plan. Returning the structured summary directly.

# Hybrid QEMU + Verilator RISC-V Co-Sim: Prior Art Summary

## (a) Most promising mechanisms

1. **dromajo cosim model** is the closest existing template. It's stepped one instruction at a time, with the RTL DUT being the master driver and dromajo serving as a golden reference. For your use case (where Verilator is not just a checker but the actual cycle-accurate execution engine for a region), the architecture inverts: dromajo->QEMU is master in the fast region, Verilator is master in the detailed region. But the cosim API shape is reusable.

2. **gem5's SimpleSwitchableProcessor** is the canonical hybrid pattern: KVM/Atomic for fast-forward, then switch to O3 at an ROI boundary triggered by a magic instruction. The same memory backend (gem5 ports) is shared between the two CPU models, so there is no memory copy at the switch. This is the architectural goal you should aim for.

3. **QEMU TCG plugin API (`qemu-plugin.h`)** is now feature-complete enough for handoff *in user-mode* and partially in softmmu:
   - `qemu_plugin_read_register()` / `qemu_plugin_write_register()` (via `qemu_plugin_register` handle obtained through `qemu_plugin_get_registers()`).
   - `qemu_plugin_read_memory_vaddr()`, `qemu_plugin_write_memory_vaddr()` for vaddr; `qemu_plugin_read_memory_hwaddr()` / `qemu_plugin_write_memory_hwaddr()` (softmmu only) for paddr.
   - `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()` and the conditional variant `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cond_cb()` give per-instruction callbacks; you filter by PC inside the callback. There is no "real" breakpoint in plugins.
   - `qemu_plugin_set_pc()` redirects execution.
   - **Gap**: there is no first-class "pause vCPU and let the host drive" primitive in plugin API. You would block inside a callback (the vCPU thread parks), then the host drains state out and resumes Verilator. The recent "stoptrigger" plugin patches show this pattern.

4. **gdbstub** as a fallback / debug bridge: full GPR/FP/CSR exposed via XML target description (`target/riscv/gdbstub.c`, `target/riscv/csr.c`); accessible programmatically over a TCP socket using GDB Remote Serial Protocol (`g`/`G`, `m`/`M`, `vCont`, `Z`/`z` packets). Useful as a sanity-check oracle but too slow for every handoff (lots of round-trips, ASCII-hex encoding).

## (b) Concrete APIs / paths

- `include/qemu/qemu-plugin.h` (TCG plugin entry points).
- `target/riscv/gdbstub.c`, `target/riscv/csr.c`, `target/riscv/cpu.h` (canonical RISC-V state).
- `target/riscv/translate.c` (TB boundaries).
- `softmmu/physmem.c`, `include/exec/ramblock.h`, `include/exec/memory.h` (RAMBlock / `memory-backend-file,share=on`).
- `migration/ram.c` + `docs/devel/migration/mapped-ram.rst` (dirty bitmap, x-ignore-shared).
- `accel/tcg/icount-common.c`, `docs/devel/tcg-icount.rst` (deterministic instruction count).
- dromajo: `include/dromajo_cosim.h` - `dromajo_cosim_step/raise_trap/override_mem`.

## (c) Recommended architecture

**Single-master-at-a-time, shared RAM.**

- **Memory**: back QEMU guest RAM with `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=/dev/shm/vsim-ram`. Verilator's RAM model `mmap`s the same fd. **No memcpy at handoff**, regardless of size. This is exactly the technique x-ignore-shared and CPR (`docs/devel/migration/CPR.rst`) use.
- **Trigger**: convention is a custom CSR write or `ecall` with a magic immediate. gem5 uses `m5op` magic NOPs decoded as no-ops on real silicon; replicate with a reserved CSR (e.g., `csrw 0x7C0, x0`) decoded by both your QEMU plugin and the Verilator decoder. tohost/fromhost is taken by HTIF/FESVR; do not reuse it.
- **Handoff**: TCG plugin callback fires on the magic insn -> reads all GPR/FP via `qemu_plugin_read_register`, reads CSRs (priv mode, `mstatus`, `satp`, `mip/mie`, `mepc`, `mtvec`, `mcause`, `mtval`, all `pmpcfg/pmpaddr`, hpmcounters) -> serializes into a small struct -> blocks the vCPU -> signals Verilator harness -> Verilator reset deasserts with that state injected through DPI hooks into the RTL register file and CSR file -> RTL runs. On return, reverse: read state out via DPI, `qemu_plugin_set_pc()` and `qemu_plugin_write_register()` to restore, unblock.
- **TLB**: do **not** try to migrate TLB contents. Both sides walk the page tables in shared RAM via `satp`; let cold misses repopulate. Same for branch predictors and caches (warmup is a separate problem, addressed by gem5's "switch + cache warming" or just running a warmup window on RTL).

## (d) Failure modes to watch

1. **MMIO/devices**: shared RAM is fine, but device state (PLIC, CLINT, UART, virtio) lives in QEMU C code with no mirror in RTL. Either (i) restrict handoff to ROI windows that touch no MMIO (the kernel-under-study assumption), or (ii) front-end QEMU's device model from RTL via a transactor (this is what FireSim does with target-side bridges; expensive).
2. **Interrupt pending bits**: must be sampled atomically with the GPRs. `mip` is partially read-only and aliased to PLIC/CLINT state; transferring it without also transferring the interrupt controllers will desync.
3. **`mcycle` / `minstret` / `time`**: not the same on both sides. Pin to a known epoch at handoff or expect the guest to observe a discontinuity. icount mode reduces drift but does not solve it across sims.
4. **Atomic / fence semantics**: QEMU TCG is sequentially consistent in a single core but A-extension reservations (`lr/sc`) are per-CPUState; reservation must be invalidated on handoff or you risk a spurious sc.success. Check `target/riscv/cpu.h:CPURISCVState::load_res`.
5. **Self-modifying code / `fence.i`**: QEMU caches translated TBs keyed on guest PC; if Verilator writes instruction memory while QEMU "owns" the CPU, you must `tb_flush` (no plugin API for it; must invalidate by re-translating, or just hand off only at quiescent points).
6. **Privilege level switches inside the ROI**: if the kernel-under-study traps to M-mode via `ecall`, the trap handler runs on Verilator at full RTL cost. Decide whether traps cause an automatic switch back to QEMU.

## (e) Andescycle tarball

Inspecting `andescycle-dev-v0.16-17-g03dfe23.tar.gz` locally: it is a corrupt/truncated gzip (`tar: Unexpected EOF`) wrapping nested `*.tar` blobs that look like Docker image layers (sha256 names). It is not directly readable. Web search shows Andes Technology ships **AndeSim** (a "near cycle" ISS bundled with AndeSight IDE) and a `andestech/qemu` GitHub fork (currently visible branch `ast-v5_4_1-release`). There is no public product called "andescycle" indexed on the web; the name and `dev-v0.16` versioning suggest an internal Andes development build, plausibly Andes' own cycle-level extension to QEMU or AndeSim packaged as a Docker image. Treating it as proprietary / internal is the safe assumption; do not plan around its exact API without inspection.

- [QEMU GDB usage](https://qemu-project.gitlab.io/qemu/system/gdb.html)
- [TCG icount](https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/devel/tcg-icount.html)
- [QEMU Record/replay](https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/devel/replay.html)
